content.problemStatement:
This time, Chef has given you an array AAA containing NNN elements.
He had also asked you to answer MMM of his questions. Each question sounds like: "How many inversions will the array AAA contain, if we swap the elements at the ithi^{th}ith and the jthj^{th}jth positions?".
The inversion is such a pair of integers (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) that i<ji < ji<j and Ai>AjA_{i} > A_{j}Ai​>Aj​.


Input Format

The first line contains two space separated integers NNN and MMM - the number of integers in the array AAA and the number of questions respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers - A1,A2,…,ANA_{1}, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{N}A1​,A2​,…,AN​, respectively.
Each of next MMM lines describes a question by two integers iii and jjj - the 1-based indices of the numbers we'd like to swap in this question.


Output Format
Output MMM lines. Output the answer to the ithi^{th}ith question of the ithi^{th}ith line.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two space-separated integers N and M - the number of integers in the array A and the number of questions respectively. The second line contains N space-separated integers - A1, A2, …, AN, respectively. Each of the next M lines describes a question by two integers i and j - the 1-based indices of the numbers we'd like to swap in this question.
content.outputFormat: Output M lines. Output the answer to the ith question of the ith line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Inversions for the first case: (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (3, 5), (4, 5). Inversions for the second case: (1, 3), (1, 5), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2,5), (4, 5). In the third case the array looks like 1 2 3 3 4 5 and there are no inversions.
The array is already sorted, so swapping any two elements will not introduce any inversions.

In the first case, after swapping the first two elements, the array becomes 4 5 3 2 1, which has inversions (2, 3), (2, 4), (3, 4), (3, 5). In the second case, after swapping the second and third elements, the array becomes 5 3 4 2 1, which has inversions (2, 3), (2, 4), (3, 4).

solution.approach: The key insight to this problem is to understand how swapping two elements in the array affects the number of inversions. We can calculate the initial number of inversions in the array and then adjust this count based on the swap. The adjustment involves considering the elements to the left and right of the swapped elements and how their relationships change.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case for the naive approach, but this can be optimized to O(N log N) or better with more efficient inversion counting methods.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array and any additional data structures needed for efficient inversion counting.
